scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plato writes from the perspective of 4th-century BCE Athens, using common social roles like cooks and guests to illustrate his epistemology.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Theaetetus is broadly dated to approximately 369 BCE, reflecting the intellectual and social norms of Plato's mature period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text discusses the specific professional standing and fees of Sophists like Protagoras within the Athenian legal and social context.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Theaetetus is broadly dated to approximately 369 BCE, documenting the social status of technical experts in Plato's time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between innate sensation and acquired reflection through 'troublesome' education reflects Athenian pedagogical ideals.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the approximate composition of the Theaetetus in 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The concept of 'good breeding' (eleutheria) and the 'gentleman' (kalos kagathos) is a specific socio-cultural construct of the Athenian elite.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the linguistic and social etiquette of the Athenian upper class around 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Socrates utilizes the Athenian legal and social category of 'insanity' to define the boundaries of cognitive reliability.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Theaetetus is broadly dated to approximately 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The examples of purchasing cloaks and owning private aviaries reflect contemporary Athenian property laws and leisure practices.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the material and legal culture of Athens during the composition of the Theaetetus around 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Oratory and law were central to the civic life of the Athenian polis, where persuasion was a primary tool of political power.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the actual social function of orators in 4th-century BCE Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The role of the dikastai (judges/jurors) and their reliance on second-hand arguments is a feature of the Athenian democratic court system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the composition of the Theaetetus, reflecting the contemporary judicial norms of 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The water-clock (klepsydra) was a specific physical and legal technology used in the Athenian law courts to regulate speech.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The use of the klepsydra was a standard feature of Athenian trials in the 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'music school' (mousike) was a standard component of the tripartite Athenian education system for free-born boys.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the educational institutions present in Athens around 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'grammatikos' (grammarian) was a recognized professional role in the Athenian educational landscape.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the occupational definitions in Athens around 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim references a common Athenian proverb regarding Mysians, reflecting local ethnic prejudices in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Theaetetus is broadly dated to approximately 369 BCE, utilizing contemporary slang and prejudices.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Socrates' mission focused on the 'noble and fair' (the elite youth) of late 5th and 4th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Theaetetus is broadly dated to approximately 369 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text reflects Athenian attitudes toward foreigners (barbaroi) and their relative standing in social interactions.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Philebus is one of Plato's late dialogues, generally dated to approximately 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The definition of educational expertise through technical knowledge (grammatike) is characteristic of 4th-century Athenian intellectual life.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the late Platonic period's emphasis on classification and number, circa 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The transition from musical practice to the status of 'musician' via theoretical mastery reflects Athenian high-culture norms.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the composition of the Philebus, circa 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion from the category of 'the wise' (sophoi) describes the social-intellectual hierachies of Plato's Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the academic standards of the Academy in Athens around 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The social obligations arising from verbal agreements in public discourse are typical of the Athenian 'culture of the word'.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the dialectical conventions of the late 4th-century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The cooperative pursuit of truth through unyielding dialectic is the foundational norm of the Socratic/Platonic community in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the composition of the Philebus, circa 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plato uses religious concepts (the goddess) to justify the imposition of law and order (nomos) in the Athenian polis.
